title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Difference between Section 80C and 80CCC
Added Word Count: 176
URL: https://www.bajajallianzlife.com/life-insurance-guide/tax/difference-between-section-80c-and-80ccc.html
The Income Tax Act,1961 of India offers various provisions to encourage individuals to save and invest, providing significant tax benefits in return. Among these, Sections 80C and 80CCC (under old tax regime) stand out as popular choices for taxpayers seeking to reduce their liabilities while securing their financial future. But what exactly sets these sections apart? What’s the real difference between 80C and 80CCC?
Section 80C: Maximizing Savings with Comprehensive Tax BenefitsSection 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, enables individual taxpayers and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) to reduce their taxable income by up to ₹1.5 lakh annually (under the old tax regime) through specified investments and expenditures. This provision encourages savings and investments among taxpayers.Eligibility:
Who Can Avail: Individual taxpayers and HUFs are eligible to claim deductions under Section 80C (under the old tax regime). Corporate entities, partnership firms, and other businesses are not eligible.
Qualifying Investments and Expenditures:
Life Insurance Premiums: Premiums paid for policies covering self, spouse, or children.
Employee Provident Fund (EPF): Employees share of Contributions made to EPF accounts.
Public Provident Fund (PPF): Deposits into PPF accounts.
National Savings Certificate (NSC): Investments in NSCs.
Equity-Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS): Investments in specified mutual funds.
Principal Repayment of Home Loans: The repayment of the actual principal component of a home loan.
Tuition Fees: Payments made towards tuition fees for up to two children to any university, college, school, or other educational institution situated within India.
Fixed Deposits: Investments in tax-saving fixed deposits with a minimum tenure of five years.
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana: Contributions to this scheme for a girl child till 21 years of age.
Senior Citizen Savings Scheme: Investments in this scheme are designed for senior citizens.
Deduction Limit:
The cumulative deduction limit under Section 80C (incase of old tax regime) is ₹1.5 lakh per financial year. This means the total amount claimed across all eligible investments and expenditures cannot exceed this threshold.By strategically investing in these instruments, you can effectively reduce your taxable income while simultaneously building a robust savings portfolio.
Section 80CCC: Encouraging Retirement Planning with Tax BenefitsSection 80CCC of the Income Tax Act, 1961, is a provision designed to promote financial security during retirement by offering tax deductions on contributions to certain pension plans. This deduction is available only under old tax regime.Eligibility:
Who Can Avail: Any individual taxpayer is eligible to claim deductions under Section 80CCC  (under old tax regime). Unlike Section 80C, this provision does not apply to Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs).
Purpose: It specifically caters to taxpayers who invest in pension or annuity plans to build a steady income stream for their retirement.
Qualifying Investments:Section 80CCC applies to contributions made (under old tax regime) towards pension funds offered by life insurance companies. These funds are designed to accumulate wealth during your working years, which is later used to provide regular income or a pension during retirement. Examples of eligible plans include:
Pension Plans from Insurers: Contributions to annuity or pension schemes, such as those offered by Bajaj Life Insurance Company or other approved insurers.
Deferred Annuity Plans: Plans that offer income at a later stage in life, ensuring financial stability during retirement years.
Deduction Limit:The maximum deduction limit under Section 80CCC is ₹1.5 lakh per financial year (under old tax regime).
This limit is includes the deduction limit of Sections 80C and 80CCD(1). In other words, the total deduction across these three sections cannot exceed ₹1.5 lakh in a financial year2(under old tax regime).
By using Section 80CCC effectively, you can not only reduce your tax liabilities but also plan for a financially secure retirement.
Understanding the Difference between 80C and 80CCCNow that we’ve explored Section 80C and Section 80CCC, understanding the distinction between the two is crucial for effective tax planning. While both sections provide valuable tax deductions, they focus on different types of investments and financial goals. Let’s dive into the difference between Section 80C and 80CCC to clarify their unique benefits and applications.
Coverage and Purpose
Section 80C: This section is broader in scope, offering deductions on a variety of investments and expenses, such as life insurance premiums, ELSS mutual funds, tuition fees, PPF, and home loan repayments. It is ideal for taxpayers looking for a wide range of options to save tax.
Section 80CCC: This section specifically focuses on contributions to pension plans or annuity schemes. It is tailored for individuals who prioritise retirement planning and wish to secure a steady income post-retirement.
Eligibility
80C: Available to individual taxpayers and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs).
80CCC: Limited to individual taxpayers only. HUFs cannot claim deductions under this section.
Tax Deduction Limit
80C: The total deduction available under this section is up to ₹1.5 lakh (under old tax regime) per financial year.
80CCC: The maximum deduction under this section is also ₹1.5 lakh (under old tax regime), but it falls under the combined ceiling of ₹1.5 lakh shared with 80C and 80CCD(1).
Tax Treatment on Returns
80C: In some cases, investments under 80C (like PPF, life insurance plans under certain terms and conditions, etc.) offer tax-free maturity benefits.
80CCC: While contributions are deductible, the pension or annuity received is taxable under the individual’s applicable income tax slab.
Key Takeaways
Section 80C allows deductions for various eligible investments and expenses like investments in PPF account, ELSS, life insurance and expenses on school fees, and repayment of home loans.
Section 80CCC allows deductions on contributions made towards approved pension plans.
Section 80C deductions can be availed by individual assessees and HUFs, whereas Section 80CCC is available only for individual assessees.
The combined deduction limit for 80C and 80CCC is ₹1.5 lakhs.
ConclusionSection 80C provides a versatile range of tax-saving instruments, while Section 80CCC is designed for retirement-centric savings through pension plans. Both sections play a vital role in financial planning, but taxpayers must balance their investments to maximise benefits while aligning with their long-term goals. By understanding the difference between 80C and 80CCC, taxpayers can make informed decisions to optimise tax savings and secure their financial future.
FAQs
What is the main difference between Section 80C and 80CCC?Section 80C offers deductions for a wide range of investments like PPF, ELSS, and life insurance premiums, while Section 80CCC is specifically for contributions to annuity plans for securing a pension.
Can I claim deductions under both 80C and 80CCC?Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections (incase of old tax regime), but the combined limit for deductions under 80C, 80CCC, and 80CCD(1) is capped at ₹1.5 lakh per financial year.
Are ELSS and pension plans covered under the same section?No, ELSS is covered under Section 80C, while pension plans that provide annuities fall under Section 80CCC.
Which section is better for long-term retirement planning?Section 80CCC is tailored for retirement planning as it supports annuity-based pension schemes2, whereas Section 80C supports a broader range of savings.
Do contributions to ULIPs fall under Section 80C or 80CCC?ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) fall under Section 80C (under old tax regime), as they combine investment and insurance benefits.
What Is the Maximum Tax Exemption Allowed Under Section 80C?
The maximum exemption under Section 80C is ₹1,50,000 per financial year1.
Which Investment Plans Qualify Under Section 80CCC?
Section 80CCC allows a deduction for money paid into an approved pension  plan (an entity approved under Section 10(23AAB)).
Who Can Claim a Deduction Under Section 80C?
Individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are allowed to make claims for deductions under Section 80C.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility
Sources
1. https://cleartax.in/s/80c-80-deductions

Difference between Section 80C and 80CCC

URL: https://www.bajajallianzlife.com/life-insurance-guide/tax/difference-between-section-80c- and-80ccc.html
The Income Tax Act,1961 of India offers various provisions to encourage individuals to save and invest, providing significant tax benefits in return. Among these, Sections 80C and 80CCC (under old tax regime) stand out as popular choices for taxpayers seeking to reduce their liabilities while securing their financial future. But what exactly sets these sections apart? What’s the real difference between 80C and 80CCC?
Section 80C: Maximizing Savings with Comprehensive Tax Benefits Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, enables individual taxpayers and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) to reduce their taxable income by up to ₹1.5 lakh annually (under the old tax regime) through specified investments and expenditures. This provision encourages savings and investments among taxpayers. Eligibility:
1. Who Can Avail: Individual taxpayers and HUFs are eligible to claim deductions under Section
80C (under the old tax regime). Corporate entities, partnership firms, and other businesses are not eligible.
Qualifying Investments and Expenditures:
2. Life Insurance Premiums: Premiums paid for policies covering self, spouse, or children.
● Employee Provident Fund (EPF): Employees share of Contributions made to EPF accounts.
● Public Provident Fund (PPF): Deposits into PPF accounts.
● National Savings Certificate (NSC): Investments in NSCs.
● Equity-Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS): Investments in specified mutual funds.
● Principal Repayment of Home Loans: The repayment of the actual principal component of a home loan.
● Tuition Fees: Payments made towards tuition fees for up to two children to any university, college, school, or other educational institution situated within India.
● Fixed Deposits: Investments in tax-saving fixed deposits with a minimum tenure of five years.
● Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana: Contributions to this scheme for a girl child till 21 years of age.
● Senior Citizen Savings Scheme: Investments in this scheme are designed for senior citizens.
Deduction Limit:
3. The cumulative deduction limit under Section 80C (incase of old tax regime) is ₹1.5 lakh per
financial year. This means the total amount claimed across all eligible investments and expenditures cannot exceed this threshold. By strategically investing in these instruments, you can effectively reduce your taxable income while simultaneously building a robust savings portfolio.
Section 80CCC: Encouraging Retirement Planning with Tax Benefits Section 80CCC of the Income Tax Act, 1961, is a provision designed to promote financial security during retirement by offering tax deductions on contributions to certain pension plans. This deduction is available only under old tax regime. Eligibility:
4. Who Can Avail: Any individual taxpayer is eligible to claim deductions under Section
80CCC (under old tax regime). Unlike Section 80C, this provision does not apply to Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs).
● Purpose: It specifically caters to taxpayers who invest in pension or annuity plans to build a steady income stream for their retirement.
Qualifying Investments: Section 80CCC applies to contributions made (under old tax regime) towards pension funds offered by life insurance companies. These funds are designed to accumulate wealth during your working years, which is later used to provide regular income or a pension during retirement. Examples of eligible plans include:
● Pension Plans from Insurers: Contributions to annuity or pension schemes, such as those offered by Bajaj Life Insurance or other approved insurers.
● Deferred Annuity Plans: Plans that offer income at a later stage in life, ensuring financial stability during retirement years.
Deduction Limit: The maximum deduction limit under Section 80CCC is ₹1.5 lakh per financial year (under old tax regime).
● This limit is includes the deduction limit of Sections 80C and 80CCD(1). In other words, the total deduction across these three sections cannot exceed ₹1.5 lakh in a financial year2(under old tax regime).
By using Section 80CCC effectively, you can not only reduce your tax liabilities but also plan for a financially secure retirement.
Understanding the Difference between 80C and 80CCC Now that we’ve explored Section 80C and Section 80CCC, understanding the distinction between the two is crucial for effective tax planning. While both sections provide valuable tax deductions, they
focus on different types of investments and financial goals. Let’s dive into the difference between Section 80C and 80CCC to clarify their unique benefits and applications.
Coverage and Purpose
1. Section 80C: This section is broader in scope, offering deductions on a variety of investments
and expenses, such as life insurance premiums, ELSS mutual funds, tuition fees, PPF, and home loan repayments. It is ideal for taxpayers looking for a wide range of options to save tax. 2. Section 80CCC: This section specifically focuses on contributions to pension plans or annuity
schemes. It is tailored for individuals who prioritise retirement planning and wish to secure a steady income post-retirement.
Eligibility
5. 80C: Available to individual taxpayers and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs).
● 80CCC: Limited to individual taxpayers only. HUFs cannot claim deductions under this section.
Tax Deduction Limit
6. 80C: The total deduction available under this section is up to ₹1.5 lakh (under old tax
regime) per financial year.
● 80CCC: The maximum deduction under this section is also ₹1.5 lakh (under old tax regime), but it falls under the combined ceiling of ₹1.5 lakh shared with 80C and 80CCD(1).
Tax Treatment on Returns
a) 80C: In some cases, investments under 80C (like PPF, life insurance plans under certain
terms and conditions, etc.) offer tax-free maturity benefits. b) 80CCC: While contributions are deductible, the pension or annuity received is taxable under
the individual’s applicable income tax slab.
Key Takeaways
1. Section 80C allows deductions for various eligible investments and expenses like
investments in PPF account, ELSS, life insurance and expenses on school fees, and repayment of home loans. 2. Section 80CCC allows deductions on contributions made towards approved pension plans. 3. Section 80C deductions can be availed by individual assessees and HUFs, whereas Section
80CCC is available only for individual assessees. 4. The combined deduction limit for 80C and 80CCC is ₹1.5 lakhs.
Conclusion Section 80C provides a versatile range of tax-saving instruments, while Section 80CCC is designed for
retirement-centric savings through pension plans. Both sections play a vital role in financial planning, but taxpayers must balance their investments to maximise benefits while aligning with their long- term goals. By understanding the difference between 80C and 80CCC, taxpayers can make informed decisions to optimise tax savings and secure their financial future.
FAQs
1. What is the main difference between Section 80C and 80CCC?
Section 80C offers deductions for a wide range of investments like PPF, ELSS, and life insurance premiums, while Section 80CCC is specifically for contributions to annuity plans for securing a pension.
2. Can I claim deductions under both 80C and 80CCC?
Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections (incase of old tax regime), but the combined limit for deductions under 80C, 80CCC, and 80CCD(1) is capped at ₹1.5 lakh per financial year.
3. Are ELSS and pension plans covered under the same section?
No, ELSS is covered under Section 80C, while pension plans that provide annuities fall under Section 80CCC.
4. Which section is better for long-term retirement planning?
Section 80CCC is tailored for retirement planning as it supports annuity-based pension schemes2, whereas Section 80C supports a broader range of savings.
5. Do contributions to ULIPs fall under Section 80C or 80CCC?
ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) fall under Section 80C (under old tax regime), as they combine investment and insurance benefits.
6. What Is the Maximum Tax Exemption Allowed Under Section 80C?
The maximum exemption under Section 80C is ₹1,50,000 per financial year1.
7. Which Investment Plans Qualify Under Section 80CCC?
Section 80CCC allows a deduction for money paid into an approved pension plan (an entity approved under Section 10(23AAB)).
8. Who Can Claim a Deduction Under Section 80C?
Individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are allowed to make claims for deductions under Section 80C.

While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering. Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. changes.word_original: What is the main difference between Section 80C and 80CCC?Section 80C offers deductions for a wide range of investments like PPF, ELSS, and life insurance premiums, while Section 80CCC is specifically for contributions to annuity plans for securing a pension.

Can I claim deductions under both 80C and 80CCC?Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections (incase of old tax regime), but the combined limit for deductions under 80C, 80CCC, and 80CCD(1) is capped at ₹1.5 lakh per financial year.
changes.pdf_final: What is the main difference between Section 80C and 80CCC?

changes.word_original: Are ELSS and pension plans covered under the same section?No, ELSS is covered under Section 80C, while pension plans that provide annuities fall under Section 80CCC.
changes.pdf_final: Section 80C offers deductions for a wide range of investments like PPF, ELSS, and life insurance premiums, while Section 80CCC is specifically for contributions to annuity plans for securing a pension.

changes.word_original: Which section is better for long-term retirement planning?Section 80CCC is tailored for retirement planning as it supports annuity-based pension schemes2, whereas Section 80C supports a broader range of savings.
Do contributions to ULIPs fall under Section 80C or 80CCC?ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) fall under Section 80C (under old tax regime), as they combine investment and insurance benefits.
changes.pdf_final: Can I claim deductions under both 80C and 80CCC?
Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections (incase of old tax regime), but the combined limit for deductions under 80C, 80CCC, and 80CCD(1) is capped at ₹1.5 lakh per financial year.
Are ELSS and pension plans covered under the same section?
No, ELSS is covered under Section 80C, while pension plans that provide annuities fall under Section 80CCC.
Which section is better for long-term retirement planning?
Section 80CCC is tailored for retirement planning as it supports annuity-based pension schemes2, whereas Section 80C supports a broader range of savings.
Do contributions to ULIPs fall under Section 80C or 80CCC?
ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) fall under Section 80C (under old tax regime), as they combine investment and insurance benefits.

changes.word_original: Section 80CCC allows a deduction for money paid into an approved pension  plan (an entity approved under Section 10(23AAB)).
changes.pdf_final: Section 80CCC allows a deduction for money paid into an approved pension plan (an entity approved under Section 10(23AAB)).
